confidence_rationale: Foundational normative anchor for what makes a behavioural hierarchy optimal -- the criterion against which option-discovery substrates should be evaluated. Solway et al. provide a mathematical account (drawing on information-bottleneck arguments) of which decompositions of a task into chunked sub-units are optimal, and behaviourally confirm in four experiments that human learners spontaneously discover those optimal hierarchies. Direct relevance for MECH-317: this is the normative target against which the various implementation flavours (option-critic, DIAYN, hand-engineered options, substrate-driven repetition triggers) should be compared. Confidence 0.80 reflects strong source quality (PLOS Comput Biol, Barto-Niv-Botvinick team) and direct relevance to the cluster question.
failure_signatures: An REE substrate that produces options which fail to match the Solway-et-al optimal hierarchy criterion will be inefficient -- the chunks won't decompose the task in the information-theoretically-correct way.

If MECH-317 produces chunks but the chunks do NOT correspond to bottleneck states or transition-rich segments of the trajectory record, the option-decomposition is unlikely to support transfer learning across related tasks.

If the substrate is producing options that match the normative-optimal criterion, behavioural validation should show humanlike spontaneous-discovery signatures (the four experiments anchored in Solway 2014 produced testable predictions about which decompositions human subjects converge on).

mapping.source_claim_statement: A behavioural hierarchy (a decomposition of a task into chunked sub-units) is optimal if it minimises the joint description-length of the task's transition structure under the hierarchical decomposition. The optimality criterion is expressible as an information-theoretic objective: the optimal hierarchy minimises the conditional entropy of the next state given the current sub-task assignment, weighted by the prior probability over sub-tasks. Behavioural experiments confirm that human learners spontaneously discover hierarchies matching this criterion on classical-conditioning and navigation paradigms, supporting the claim that optimal-hierarchy discovery is what brains actually do (not just what they could in principle do).
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-317's option-discovery substrate should produce options that approximate Solway-et-al optimal hierarchies. The information-theoretic criterion specifies which decompositions are 'good' for a given task structure -- specifically, chunks should align with bottleneck states (low-entropy transitions in the trajectory record) and with persistent sub-tasks (transition clusters that maintain internal coherence). For REE's substrate-design pass, this anchors a normative target: the implementation flavour chosen (option-critic gradient, DIAYN diversity, substrate-driven repetition) should be compared against the Solway criterion to determine whether the produced chunks are optimal-in-the-right-sense.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Solway et al.'s analysis is normative -- it shows that optimal hierarchies are well-defined and that humans can find them in specific paradigms. The substrate-level implementation that produces optimal hierarchies is not directly prescribed; the four experiments tested behaviour rather than substrate. The paper is best read as defining the NORMATIVE TARGET for option-discovery, against which different implementation flavours can be compared. Whether REE's specific behavioural substrate (SD-054 reef foraging) has well-defined optimal hierarchies in the Solway sense is a substrate-readiness question; the answer depends on the bottleneck-state structure of the trajectory record. SD-054's reef-approach-feed-retreat sequence may have clearly-defined bottleneck states; SD-054-extended-with-multi-rule-context environments definitely will.
mapping.source_context: PLOS Comput Biol 10(8):e1003779 (2014). Princeton/UMass team (Solway, Diuk, Cordova, Yee, Barto, Niv, Botvinick) -- broad authorship spanning the Niv-Botvinick research programme. Normative analysis + four behavioural experiments confirming human spontaneous discovery of optimal hierarchies.

confidence_components.notes: PLOS Comput Biol is high-quality; Barto-Niv-Botvinick team is foundational. Mapping fidelity strong for the normative criterion. Transfer risk low for the criterion itself; somewhat elevated for whether REE's specific trajectory-record substrate has well-defined optimal hierarchies, which is substrate-dependent.
